perturbation.original_text: (c) “ Confidential Information ” shall mean all customer and supplier lists, marketing arrangements, business plans, projections, financial information, training manuals, pricing manuals, product development plans, market strategies, internal performance statistics and other competitively sensitive information of Seller used solely in the Business and not generally known by the public, whether or not in written or tangible form. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the definition of Confidential Information shall not include any of the foregoing items insofar as they relate to Seller Exclusive Products.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) “ Confidential Information ” shall mean all customer and supplier lists, marketing arrangements, business plans, projections, financial information, training manuals, pricing manuals, product development plans, market strategies, internal performance statistics and other competitively sensitive information of Seller used solely in the Business and not generally known by the public, whether or not in written or tangible form. **Information shared with consultants acting on behalf of the business is also considered confidential.** Notwithstanding the foregoing, the definition of Confidential Information shall not include any of the foregoing items insofar as they relate to Seller Exclusive Products.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'Information shared with consultants acting on behalf of the business is also considered confidential,' we expand the scope of 'Confidential Information.' This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 4, 'No Disclosure of Proprietary Information,' if consultants were previously considered outside the bounds of confidentiality. The original definition focuses on information 'not generally known by the public,' implying internal-only data. Including consultant-shared data blurs this line and makes enforcement unclear if such consultants are not directly employees or bound by the agreement, leading to In-Text Contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, it will not, directly or indirectly, disclose to anyone, or use or otherwise exploit for its own benefit or for the benefit of anyone other than Purchaser, any Confidential Information, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller may make disclosure of Confidential Information if Seller reasonably concludes that public disclosure of Confidential Information is required by applicable legal requirements and Seller (i) gives Purchaser written notice of such proposed disclosure as far in advance of such disclosure as is reasonably practicable, (ii) cooperates reasonably with Purchaser in its efforts to protect the information from disclosure, including, without limitation, assisting Purchaser in obtaining, at Purchaser’s expense, an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such information, and (iii) limits its disclosure to the minimum required by applicable legal requirements unless Purchaser agrees in writing to a greater level of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The change expands the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include information shared with consultants. The contradicted text, Section 4, restricts the Seller from disclosing 'Confidential Information' to 'anyone.' Originally, the scope of 'anyone' arguably excluded consultants. Now, with consultants explicitly included in the definition of 'Confidential Information,' a clear contradiction arises. The Seller is now explicitly prohibited from disclosing confidential information to consultants, despite the fact that they might need to share that information. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the new definition conflicts with the disclosure restrictions.

perturbation.original_text: (d) “ Infant and Toddler Retail Market ” shall mean those retail sales departments within retailers which sell products intended for children from ages zero to four years of age.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) “ Infant and Toddler Retail Market ” shall mean those retail sales departments within retailers which sell products intended for children from ages zero to four years of age **and which generate at least 50% of their revenue from the sale of such products.**
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'and which generate at least 50% of their revenue from the sale of such products' narrows the definition of 'Infant and Toddler Retail Market.' Section 2, 'No Competing Business,' now contains contradictory definitions. Before the perturbation, the restrictive covenant applied to anyone selling in the retail market for infants and toddlers, but now it only applies to those sellers that get at least 50% of their revenue from sales within that market.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, own, manage, operate, control, invest in or acquire an interest in, or otherwise engage or participate in the establishment, management or operation of, any Competitive Business that sells or distributes Competitive Products, directly or indirectly, for ultimate purchase by consumers in the Protected Market, without regard to whether the Competitive Business has any office, manufacturing or other business facilities within the Protected Market.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a revenue requirement to the definition of "Infant and Toddler Retail Market". This narrows the scope of what constitutes that market. The contradicted text broadly restricts the Seller from engaging in any Competitive Business that sells Competitive Products in the Protected Market. The change creates a contradiction because the original text restricted the seller from participating in any business in the infant and toddler retail market, whereas the changed text only restricts the seller from those businesses that get at least 50% of their revenue from sales in that market. This allows the seller to participate in businesses that sell infant and toddler products, as long as they generate less than 50% of their revenue from them, which contradicts the original intent of the non-compete clause.

perturbation.original_text: Seller represents and warrants that this Agreement is a legal, valid and binding obligation, enforceable against Seller in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller represents and warrants that this Agreement is a legal, valid and binding obligation, enforceable against Seller in accordance with its terms, **except for clauses pertaining to non-compete activities, which are subject to reasonable interpretation based on prevailing market conditions.**
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'except for clauses pertaining to non-compete activities, which are subject to reasonable interpretation based on prevailing market conditions,' we introduce ambiguity into Section 6. The original clause provided an unequivocal guarantee of enforceability. Now, non-compete activities have a caveat, contradicting the initial, absolute warranty. This creates In-Text Contradiction, as it suggests that not all clauses are fully enforceable, undermining the general warranty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller represents and warrants that this Agreement is a legal, valid and binding obligation, enforceable against Seller in accordance with its terms, except for clauses pertaining to non-compete activities, which are subject to reasonable interpretation based on prevailing market conditions.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement is enforceable against the seller according to its terms. The changed text adds an exception for non-compete clauses, subjecting them to 'reasonable interpretation' based on market conditions. This creates a direct contradiction, as it weakens the original guarantee of enforceability. The contradicted text also states the agreement is enforceable but includes an exception regarding non-compete activities, which makes the overall agreement's enforceability unclear. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists as defined by 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement, and supersedes all other prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, among the parties, or any of them with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement, and supersedes all other prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, among the parties, or any of them with respect to the subject matter hereof, **except for any implied agreements or understandings arising from established business practices prior to the effective date of this agreement.**
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'except for any implied agreements or understandings arising from established business practices prior to the effective date of this agreement,' we create an exception to the 'Entire Agreement' clause (Section 15). This introduces ambiguity and In-Text Contradiction. While the original clause explicitly states that the agreement supersedes all prior understandings, the added exception reintroduces the possibility that prior (and unwritten) business practices may still hold weight. This makes it unclear what truly constitutes the complete agreement between the parties and could allow for disputes based on past practices.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement, and supersedes all other prior agreements and understandings, both written and oral, among the parties, or any of them with respect to the subject matter hereof, except for any implied agreements or understandings arising from established business practices prior to the effective date of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the 'Entire Agreement' clause, allowing for implied agreements based on past business practices to be considered. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text. The original text stated there were no exceptions. The addition creates a direct conflict, as it contradicts the original intent of the entire agreement clause and leads to uncertainty about what constitutes the complete agreement.
